Faith in God's promises should not lessen our diligence in using appropriate methods. God’s providence directed the spies to Rahab’s house. God knew where there was someone who would be loyal to them, even though they did not. Rahab appears to have been an innkeeper; and if she had previously lived a questionable life, which is doubtful, she had abandoned her bad habits. What seems most accidental to us is often controlled by Divine providence to serve great purposes. It was by faith that Rahab received those with peace, against whom her king and country were at war. We are sure this was a good work; the apostle speaks of it as such, and she did it by faith, a faith that put her above fear of people. Only those who find in their hearts to risk everything for God are true believers; they consider God’s people as their own, and join with them. The spies were led by God's special providence, and Rahab entertained them out of regard for Israel and Israel's God, not for money or any evil purpose. Although excuses may be offered for Rahab's falsehood, it seems best to admit nothing that tends to explain it away. Her understanding of the Divine law must have been very limited; a lie like this, told by those who enjoy the light of revelation, whatever the motive, would deserve strong condemnation.

Rahab had heard of the miracles the Lord performed for Israel. She believed that his promises would certainly be fulfilled and his threats carried out; and that there was no way to escape except by submitting to him and joining with his people. Rahab’s actions proved that she possessed genuine Divine faith. Observe the promises the spies made to her. God's goodness is often expressed through his kindness and truth; in both of these we must be followers of him. Those who are careful to keep promises are cautious about making them. The spies set forth necessary conditions. The scarlet cord, like the blood upon the doorpost at Passover, reminds us of a sinner’s security under Christ's atoning blood; and that we should flee to it for refuge from the wrath of a justly offended God. The same cord Rahab used to save these Israelites was to be used for her own safety. What we serve and honor God with, we may expect he will bless and make useful to us.

The report the spies brought was encouraging. All the people of the country are discouraged because of Israel; they have neither wisdom to surrender nor courage to fight. Those terrors of conscience and that sense of Divine wrath, which dismay the ungodly but do not lead them to repentance, are frightening signs of approaching destruction. But grace still abounds for even the chief of sinners. Let them flee to Christ without delay, and all will be well.
